subcategory: iconic orange-blue crane-flower South African perennial

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants or rhizome divisions in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; bird of paradise is a slow-growing perennial from the Eastern Cape of South Africa requiring patience — container-grown transplants typically take 2–4 years to reach first flowering, and seed-grown plants 5–7 years. Once established, plants are drought-tolerant and long-lived, blooming continuously for decades. Overcrowded clumps flower best — do not divide unnecessarily. Full sun is essential for flowering; part shade produces foliage but few flowers.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; bird of paradise is one of the most internationally recognized flowering plants in horticulture — the floral emblem of Los Angeles, a staple of tropical-theme gardens worldwide, and one of the most distinctive and immediately identifiable flowers produced by any plant. The flowers emerge from a horizontal, boat-shaped, green-purple spathe (the 'beak' of the 'bird') on a tall, stiff scape held above the large, oval, paddle-shaped, blue-green leaves on long petioles. The flower itself consists of three vivid, saturated-orange sepals arranged in a fan and three blue-purple petals — two fused into a pointed, arrow-shaped structure that projects forward — the overall composition creating an unmistakable resemblance to the head and crest of a tropical bird, complete with orange 'crown' and blue 'beak'. The flower's elaborate structure is adapted for pollination by sunbirds in its native South Africa: the weight of the bird on the blue petal structure exposes the pollen-covered anthers that dust the bird's feet. Native to the coastal and riverine scrub of the Eastern Cape of South Africa, it grows in full sun, lean, well-drained soil, and tolerates considerable drought when established. In zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens it is a reliable, long-lived, repeating-bloom perennial that produces its extraordinary flowers throughout the year when mature.

subcategory: fragrant cream-yellow tropical ginger, shade perennial

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in part shade to full sun; yellow butterfly ginger is nearly identical to white butterfly ginger (H. coronarium) in garden habits and cold hardiness but produces cream-yellow to pale-yellow flowers with a yellow labellum rather than pure white. In zone 9 it is a reliable, returning perennial from underground rhizomes. It has naturalized in Hawaii, Florida, and other subtropical areas. Slightly more vigorous and faster-spreading than H. coronarium. Provides several weeks of fragrant summer bloom.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in part shade to full sun; yellow butterfly ginger is the cream-yellow counterpart of the iconic white butterfly ginger (Hedychium coronarium) — a closely related species from the Himalayan foothills of India and Nepal producing in summer and early autumn terminal spikes of sweetly fragrant, butterfly-shaped flowers in pale cream-yellow to soft butter-yellow with a contrasting deeper-yellow labellum (central lip), the whole flower having the characteristic hedychium butterfly silhouette of narrow upper petals and a broad, spreading lower lip. Like H. coronarium, it is powerfully fragrant, the sweet, warm, somewhat vanilla-like scent carrying through the garden on summer evenings. The growth habit, cultural requirements, and winter hardiness are nearly identical to H. coronarium — erect rhizomatous clumps reaching 5–7 feet with large, lance-shaped, fresh-green ginger-family leaves, dying back to the ground in cold winters and resprouting vigorously in spring. It has naturalized in Hawaii, the Florida panhandle, and other subtropical Atlantic and Gulf Coast areas. The cream-yellow flower color provides a warm, soft alternative to the pure white of H. coronarium in gardens where a slightly warmer color palette is desired. Outstanding as a fragrant, tropical-character shade and part-shade perennial for zone 7–11 moist gardens.

subcategory: fruiting banana, tropical perennial for zone 9-11

planting.note: Plant rooted suckers (pups) in moist, well-drained, richly fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; fruiting banana requires deep, fertile, consistently moist soil and full sun for fruit production. In zone 9a it may suffer pseudostem dieback in hard winters but regrows from the rhizome — however, fruit production requires 10–14 months of uninterrupted warm-season growth. In zone 9b and 10 it fruits reliably. Feed heavily with a high-potassium fertilizer monthly. Each pseudostem fruits once then dies; new suckers take over. Provide wind protection for the large leaves.

directions: Plant rooted suckers (pups) in moist, well-drained, richly fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; common banana is the species underlying the world's most important fruit crop — the seedless, triploid Cavendish and related commercial cultivars that supply the global banana market, all derived from the diploid wild species Musa acuminata native to tropical Southeast Asia. In zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens, selected cultivars including 'Dwarf Cavendish' (6–8 feet), 'Grand Nain' (the commercial Cavendish, 8–12 feet), and 'Goldfinger' produce genuine, edible, sweet bananas from plants grown in-ground as perennials. Each pseudostem (the apparent trunk, composed of tightly overlapping leaf-base sheaths) grows for 10–14 months, then produces a pendant inflorescence from which the banana bunch develops — the large, pendulous, purple-red flower bract opening to reveal successive 'hands' of bananas along the central axis. After fruiting, the fruited pseudostem dies and is cut to the ground; the continuously produced suckers (pups) at the base replace it, maintaining the clump and producing the next generation of fruiting pseudostems. The plant requires consistently rich, moist soil and heavy feeding with high-potassium fertilizer during the growing season for optimum fruit production. In zone 9a the pseudostems may be killed back to the ground by hard freezes but the rhizome resprouts in spring; however, this resets the 10–14 month fruiting clock, often preventing fruit set before the next winter. In zone 9b and 10 the pseudostems persist year-round and fruit reliably.
